angle: Extending the memory write-time quality-loop scorer with a corpus-grounding/distinctiveness signal that rides the persisted memory row into retrieval, so the requestQuality gate can distinguish corpus-grounded matches from off-corpus spurious cosine hits (e.g. the kubernetes-at-0.78 false-good).

Extending the memory write-time quality-loop scorer with a corpus-grounding/distinctiveness signal that rides the persisted memory row into retrieval, so the requestQuality gate can distinguish corpus-grounded matches from off-corpus spurious cosine hits (e.g. the kubernetes-at-0.78 false-good).
title: Add a write-time distinctiveness sub-score and persist it as a queryable retrieval prior
problem: The quality-loop scorer measures only four structural dimensions — triggers (0.25), anchors (0.30), budget (0.20), coherence (0.25) — none of which captures semantic distinctiveness/term-rarity (handlers/quality-loop.ts:74-79 QUALITY_WEIGHTS; sub-scorers at :103, :137, :235, :274). Confirmed: nothing on the write side carries a corpus-grounding signal. Meanwhile the retrieval-time requestQuality verdict (lib/search/confidence-scoring.ts:433-485 assessRequestQuality) is driven PURELY by topScore = resolveCalibrationScore(results[0]) (absolute cosine, :453), with TOP_DOMINANT_THRESHOLD=0.8 (:78) and HIGH_THRESHOLD=0.7 (:39). It reads ZERO write-time signal — which is exactly why an off-corpus term hitting a doc at cosine 0.78 clears the gate as 'good'. The plumbing to fix this already exists: quality_score IS persisted on the row (handlers/save/create-record.ts:389) and PipelineRow carries quality_score + quality_flags (lib/search/pipeline/types.ts:41-43), so anything the write-time scorer stamps onto the row is already visible to confidence-scoring's ScoredResult (Record<string,unknown>) at retrieval — for free, no schema plumbing through the 4-stage pipeline.
evidence: handlers/quality-loop.ts:74-79 (QUALITY_WEIGHTS, four dims); :392-422 (computeMemoryQualityScore aggregation); handlers/save/create-record.ts:389-390 (quality_score/quality_flags persisted to row); lib/search/pipeline/types.ts:41-43 (PipelineRow carries quality_score/quality_flags); lib/search/confidence-scoring.ts:453 + 471-484 (verdict purely from cosine topScore); lib/search/bm25-index.ts:604 (calculateIdf corpus-IDF surface already exists); lib/validation/save-quality-gate.ts:459-487 (scoreTitleQuality — existing specificity-scoring precedent, generic-title penalty).
proposal: Add a fifth sub-scorer scoreDistinctiveness(content, metadata) to handlers/quality-loop.ts that measures how lexically specific/grounded the memory's own key terms are against the live corpus, reusing the existing corpus-IDF surface BM25Index.calculateIdf (lib/search/bm25-index.ts:604) rather than inventing a new statistic: take the memory's trigger phrases + heading terms, look up their corpus IDF, and emit a [0,1] specificity score (high IDF / rare-but-present terms = distinctive; all-generic terms = low). Persist this as a NEW dedicated row column (e.g. distinctiveness_score) written alongside quality_score in create-record.ts:389, NOT folded into the composite total (folding it would change the gate threshold semantics and every existing test in quality-loop.vitest.ts). Keep it additive and flag-gated. This is the load-bearing primitive the other proposals build on.
benefit: Creates the first corpus-grounding signal computed at write time and carried to retrieval on the same row, enabling the requestQuality gate to later down-weight off-corpus matches. Reuses an existing IDF statistic (no new corpus index), and because it rides the already-persisted-and-already-on-PipelineRow channel, it needs no plumbing through the 4 pipeline stages.
risk: BM25Index is a per-call/rebuildable index; computing corpus-IDF at every memory_save adds latency or requires a warm shared index — if the corpus index isn't available at save time the scorer must degrade to neutral (0.5) not block the save. A new DB column needs a migration; mis-handling absent values (NULL) must read as neutral, not as 'not distinctive'.

angle: Extending the memory write-time quality-loop scorer with a corpus-grounding/distinctiveness signal that rides the persisted memory row into retrieval, so the requestQuality gate can distinguish corpus-grounded matches from off-corpus spurious cosine hits (e.g. the kubernetes-at-0.78 false-good).
title: Make requestQuality 'good' require a corpus-grounding floor, not cosine alone
problem: assessRequestQuality returns 'good' as soon as topScore >= TOP_DOMINANT_THRESHOLD (0.8) OR (topScore >= HIGH_THRESHOLD 0.7 AND head mostly confident/dominant) — lib/search/confidence-scoring.ts:471-478. topScore is resolveCalibrationScore(results[0]) = absolute cosine only (:453). This is the precise code path that waved through the off-corpus 'kubernetes' citation: a single high cosine number is sufficient for 'good', and citationPolicy cites iff good (per the phase-029 finding). There is no second, orthogonal gate asking 'is this query term actually grounded in this doc's distinctive vocabulary?'.
evidence: lib/search/confidence-scoring.ts:471-484 (the exact good/weak/gap decision, cosine-only inputs); :453 (topScore = resolveCalibrationScore = cosine); :78 TOP_DOMINANT_THRESHOLD=0.8, :39 HIGH_THRESHOLD=0.7, :40 LOW_THRESHOLD=0.4 (the 0.8/0.7/0.4 thresholds the phase-029 finding names); :170-174 (existing flag-gated calibration toggle pattern to mirror).
proposal: Gate the 'good' verdict with a second condition that reads the top result's persisted distinctiveness_score (from proposal 1) off the row — available because PipelineRow carries arbitrary row columns (lib/search/pipeline/types.ts:15, Record<string,unknown>). Concretely: when the top hit's cosine is in the 0.7-0.85 'confident-but-not-overwhelming' band AND its distinctiveness signal is low, demote 'good' to 'weak' (do-not-cite). Leave the >=0.8 dominant path and high-distinctiveness path untouched so genuine grounded hits still read 'good'. Implement behind a flag (mirror the existing SPECKIT_ABSOLUTE_RELEVANCE_CALIBRATION graduated-default pattern at confidence-scoring.ts:170-174) so it can be A/B'd against the phase-029 144-cell baseline before defaulting on.
benefit: Directly closes the spurious-citation hole: the off-corpus kubernetes hit at 0.78 would fall in the demotion band and read 'weak' (no citation) while 'authentication' at 0.50 already stays weak and genuine grounded hits at high distinctiveness stay 'good'. Turns the cite-iff-good policy (already honored at rate 1.0 by every model) into a meaningfully safer gate without touching model fidelity.
risk: Two-sided error: too aggressive a demotion suppresses legitimate citations (recall loss) — must be tuned against the phase-029 fixtures, not shipped default-on blind. Distinctiveness must be present on the row; for memories saved before proposal 1's column exists it reads NULL → the gate must treat absent-distinctiveness as 'do not demote' (fail-open to current behavior) to avoid regressing the whole pre-existing corpus.

angle: Extending the memory write-time quality-loop scorer with a corpus-grounding/distinctiveness signal that rides the persisted memory row into retrieval, so the requestQuality gate can distinguish corpus-grounded matches from off-corpus spurious cosine hits (e.g. the kubernetes-at-0.78 false-good).
title: Persist the per-dimension breakdown (and the new distinctiveness sub-score) instead of discarding it to eval_run_id=0
problem: computeMemoryQualityScore returns a rich per-dimension breakdown {triggers, anchors, budget, coherence} (handlers/quality-loop.ts:407-414), but only the scalar total survives onto the memory row (memory-save.ts:532 parsed.qualityScore = qualityLoopResult.score.total). The breakdown is written ONLY to eval_metric_snapshots with eval_run_id=0 (quality-loop.ts:720-739) — an eval/telemetry sink, not joinable back to the memory row. So at retrieval time the row exposes a single opaque 0-1 number; you cannot tell a 0.7 that is 0.7 because of weak triggers from a 0.7 that is weak on coherence. A distinctiveness sub-score (proposal 1) would be the most retrieval-relevant dimension and would suffer the same fate if simply folded into the total.
evidence: handlers/quality-loop.ts:407-414 (breakdown computed); :720-739 (breakdown logged to eval_metric_snapshots, eval_run_id=0, hardcoded at :733); handlers/memory-save.ts:532 (only .score.total persisted to parsed.qualityScore); handlers/save/create-record.ts:390 (quality_flags already JSON-serialized to the row); lib/search/pipeline/types.ts:42-43 (qualityFlags/quality_flags already on PipelineRow).
proposal: Stamp the structured breakdown (and the distinctiveness sub-score) onto the row as a compact JSON column or reuse the existing quality_flags JSON channel (already written at create-record.ts:390, already on PipelineRow at pipeline/types.ts:42-43) rather than only emitting it to the eval table. Minimal version: extend the quality_flags payload to include the numeric breakdown, since it is already JSON-serialized and already round-trips to retrieval. This makes the per-dimension signal — especially distinctiveness — inspectable by the retrieval gate and by /memory:search diagnostics without a new schema migration.
benefit: Unlocks proposals 1-2 cheaply: the distinctiveness sub-score reaches retrieval via the existing quality_flags JSON channel with no new column/migration, and the gate can read the specific dimension it needs. Also makes the breakdown debuggable for the phase-029-style calibration audits that exposed the 0.78 false-good.
risk: quality_flags is currently a string[] of human-readable issue strings consumed elsewhere; overloading it with numeric breakdown data could break any consumer that assumes it is a flat string array — must verify all readers (grep showed no search-side consumers, but save/dedup.ts:103 selects quality_flags) tolerate a richer shape, or use a separate column instead. Low blast radius but needs a consumer-compatibility check before shipping.
